meta.title: Baby Dragon Hatchling (BDH)
meta.description: A brain-inspired post-transformer architecture with Hebbian learning, synaptic memory, and scale-free hub routing
meta.author: Peter Balogh

props.title: 1. Why BDH Exists: The Transformer's Limitations

children.children: Transformers revolutionized AI, but they have fundamental constraints baked into their design. Weights are frozen after training — the model can't learn anything new at inference time. Attention is global and quadratic — every token attends to every other token at O(N²) cost. And the context window is a fixed-size conveyor belt: once tokens fall off, they're gone.

Baby Dragon Hatchling (BDH) is a proposed post-transformer architecture that claims to address all three of these limitations simultaneously. It replaces frozen weights with Hebbian plasticity (connections that change during inference), replaces the KV-cache with synaptic state living on graph edges, and replaces O(N²) attention with O(N) linear attention mapping.

Important caveat: BDH is early-stage research. As of 2025, it has not been validated on major benchmarks, and its claims about surpassing transformers at scale remain unproven. This tutorial presents the architecture's ideas, not established results. Read with scientific skepticism.

props.title: Coral Reef vs. Factory Assembly Line

children.children: A transformer is like a factory assembly line. The machinery (weights) is fixed. Raw materials (tokens) move along a conveyor belt (context window) through predetermined stations (attention heads). The factory is incredibly efficient at what it was designed to produce — but it can't redesign itself while running.

BDH is more like a living coral reef. The reef (graph) is constantly growing and reshaping itself. New connections form where activity is high. Old, unused pathways weaken and dissolve. The structure IS the memory. There's no separate conveyor belt — the reef itself remembers what has passed through it.

props.title: 2. Neuron Particles: Excitatory and Inhibitory Units

children.children: BDH's basic computational units aren't transformer neurons with simple activation functions. They're 'neuron particles' modeled on biological integrate-and-fire neurons. Each neuron accumulates input over time and only fires when it crosses a threshold — just like real neurons.

There are two types: excitatory neurons (which push other neurons toward firing) and inhibitory neurons (which push them away from firing). This push-pull dynamic creates natural competition and sparsity — only the relevant clusters of neurons fire for any given input, not the entire network.

# Simplified neuron particle update
membrane_potential = 0.0
threshold = 1.0
decay = 0.95

for t in range(timesteps):
    # Accumulate weighted inputs from connected neurons
    input_current = sum(w_ij * x_j for j in connected_neurons)
    
    # Integrate with leak (decay)
    membrane_potential = decay * membrane_potential + input_current
    
    # Fire if threshold is crossed
    if membrane_potential >= threshold:
        spike = 1.0          # Neuron fires!
        membrane_potential = 0.0  # Reset after firing
    else:
        spike = 0.0          # Silent this timestep

# Key difference from transformers:
# - Not all neurons compute every step (sparse activation)
# - Excitatory neurons: input_current > 0 (promotes firing)
# - Inhibitory neurons: input_current < 0 (suppresses firing)

Sparse activation is the key efficiency claim. In a transformer, every token activates every attention head and every feedforward neuron. In BDH, only the relevant neuron clusters fire — like how your brain doesn't activate the visual cortex when you're listening to a podcast. This could mean far less compute per inference step, IF the routing works well.

children.title: Biological Plausibility: How Close Is This to Real Neurons?

children.children: Real biological neurons are vastly more complex than BDH's neuron particles. They have dendritic trees with their own local computation, dozens of neurotransmitter types, glial cell interactions, and timing-dependent plasticity at the millisecond scale. BDH's integrate-and-fire model is a dramatic simplification — but it captures two properties that transformers completely lack:

First, temporal integration — neurons accumulate evidence over time before committing to a decision (firing). This is fundamentally different from the single-pass feedforward computation of a transformer layer. Second, lateral inhibition — inhibitory neurons create winner-take-all competition, naturally selecting the most relevant representation without needing softmax normalization over the entire sequence.

Whether these biologically-inspired properties actually improve language modeling performance over transformers is an open empirical question. Many 'brain-inspired' architectures have been proposed over the decades; few have matched the practical effectiveness of transformers on large-scale tasks.

props.title: 3. Synaptic State (σ): Memory Lives on the Edges

children.children: In a transformer, intermediate memory is stored in the KV-cache — a separate data structure that grows linearly with sequence length and gets discarded between sessions. In BDH, there is no separate memory store. Memory lives ON the connections between neurons, encoded as synaptic state σ.

Each edge in BDH's graph carries a synaptic state that includes the connection weight, a plasticity factor (how easily this connection changes), and recent activity history. When the network processes input, these edge states are modified in real time. The graph structure IS the memory.

# Transformer KV-Cache (separate, external memory)
kv_cache = {}  # Grows with each token
for token in sequence:
    k, v = compute_key_value(token)  # Project token
    kv_cache[pos] = (k, v)           # Store externally
    # At end of session: kv_cache is DISCARDED
    # Next session starts from scratch

# BDH Synaptic State (memory ON the edges)
class SynapticEdge:
    weight: float        # Connection strength
    plasticity: float    # How easily this edge changes
    trace: float         # Recent activity history (eligibility trace)
    
    def update(self, pre_activity, post_activity, learning_rate):
        # Edge MODIFIES ITSELF during inference
        delta = learning_rate * self.plasticity * pre_activity * post_activity
        self.weight += delta
        self.trace = 0.9 * self.trace + pre_activity * post_activity

# Key difference: synaptic state PERSISTS and EVOLVES
# No separate cache to manage or discard

A transformer's KV-cache is like carrying a notebook. You write down everything you see, flip back through pages to recall things, and throw the notebook away at the end of the day. If the notebook gets too long, you can't read the early pages anymore (context window limit).

BDH's synaptic state is like footpaths through a forest. Every time you walk a route, the path gets a little more worn. Frequently-used paths become wide trails. Neglected paths grow over. You never 'write down' where you've been — the landscape itself records your history. And it never 'fills up' the way a notebook does.

props.title: 4. Hebbian Learning: Connections That Change During Inference

children.children: The most radical claim of BDH is that learning happens not just during training, but continuously during inference. The mechanism is Hebbian learning — the oldest and simplest learning rule in neuroscience: 'neurons that fire together wire together.'
The mathematical formulation is straightforward:

Δw_ij = η · x_i · y_j

Where:
  Δw_ij = change in connection weight between neuron i and neuron j
  η     = learning rate (how fast connections change)
  x_i   = activity of the pre-synaptic neuron (sender)
  y_j   = activity of the post-synaptic neuron (receiver)

Interpretation:
  - If both neurons are active (x_i > 0, y_j > 0): weight INCREASES
    → 'Neurons that fire together wire together'
  - If one is active but the other isn't: small or no change
  - The more strongly both fire, the bigger the weight change

Example with numbers:
  η = 0.01, x_i = 0.8 (sender fires strongly), y_j = 0.6 (receiver fires)
  Δw_ij = 0.01 × 0.8 × 0.6 = 0.0048
  → Connection strengthened by 0.48%

  Next time, if x_i = 0.8, y_j = 0.0 (receiver silent):
  Δw_ij = 0.01 × 0.8 × 0.0 = 0.0
  → No change — they didn't fire together

props.title: Hebbian Learning Simulator
props.description: Watch how connection strength changes as two neurons fire (or don't fire) over multiple timesteps. When both fire together, the connection strengthens. When they fire independently, it weakens.

children.children:
Step-by-step Hebbian learning between Neuron A and Neuron B:

  Step │ A fires? │ B fires? │  Δw      │  Weight  │ Visual
  ─────┼──────────┼──────────┼──────────┼──────────┼────────────────────
    0  │          │          │          │  0.500   │ A ───── B
    1  │   ✓ 0.9  │   ✓ 0.7  │ +0.0063  │  0.506   │ A ═════ B  (stronger!)
    2  │   ✓ 0.8  │   ✓ 0.8  │ +0.0064  │  0.513   │ A ══════ B
    3  │   ✗ 0.1  │   ✓ 0.9  │ +0.0009  │  0.514   │ A ══════ B (barely changed)
    4  │   ✓ 0.9  │   ✓ 0.6  │ +0.0054  │  0.519   │ A ═══════ B
    5  │   ✗ 0.0  │   ✗ 0.0  │  0.0000  │  0.519   │ A ═══════ B (no change)
    6  │   ✓ 0.7  │   ✓ 0.8  │ +0.0056  │  0.525   │ A ════════ B
    7  │   ✓ 0.9  │   ✗ 0.1  │ +0.0009  │  0.525   │ A ════════ B
    8  │   ✓ 0.8  │   ✓ 0.9  │ +0.0072  │  0.533   │ A ═════════ B

  η = 0.01 │ Formula: Δw = η × A_activity × B_activity

  Key insight: co-firing drives strengthening.
  Steps 3, 5, 7 show: when one neuron is inactive, the weight barely moves.
  Steps 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 show: mutual activity = rapid strengthening.

  Over time, frequently co-active pathways become 'highways'
  while unused connections remain weak 'dirt roads.'

children.title: The Stability Problem: Runaway Hebbian Learning

children.children: Pure Hebbian learning has a well-known failure mode: positive feedback loops. If neuron A strengthens its connection to neuron B, B fires more easily, which further strengthens the A→B connection, which makes B fire even more easily... weights grow without bound.

Biological brains solve this with homeostatic mechanisms: synaptic scaling (global normalization of connection weights), metaplasticity (the plasticity rate itself adapts), and inhibitory interneurons (which create competitive pressure). BDH proposes analogous mechanisms, but whether they're sufficient to maintain stable operation over long inference sessions — thousands or millions of steps — is one of the architecture's biggest open questions.

Transformers sidestep this entirely by having fixed weights during inference. It's boring but it's stable. BDH's ambition to learn during inference is exciting but comes with engineering risks that haven't been stress-tested at scale.

Hebbian learning during inference means BDH could, in theory, adapt to new patterns in real time — learning a user's vocabulary, adjusting to a domain, or building associations between concepts it has never seen before. No fine-tuning, no retraining. The model literally rewires itself as it runs. Whether this works reliably is another matter entirely.

props.title: 5. Scale-Free Hub Router: Six Degrees of Separation

children.children: BDH organizes its neurons not as uniform layers (like transformers) but as a scale-free graph — a network where most nodes have just a few connections, but a small number of 'hub' nodes connect to thousands. This is the same topology as the internet, social networks, and the brain's white matter tracts.

The hubs serve as routers. When information enters the network at one location, it can reach any other node in just a few hops by passing through hubs — like how you can reach anyone on Earth through about six social connections. This enables a form of logical reasoning: if hub H knows that A→B (modus ponens), and a signal arrives saying 'A is true,' H can rapidly route the conclusion 'therefore B' to the relevant part of the network.

props.title: Scale-Free Graph with Hub Routing
props.description: A scale-free network where hub nodes (large circles) connect clusters. Trace how a signal can reach any node in 2-3 hops through hubs, vs. 10+ hops without them.

children.children:
Scale-Free Network Topology (30 nodes, 3 hubs):

    [a]─[b]─[c]              [p]─[q]
     │       │                 │
    [d]──────┤     [m]─[n]────[r]
             │      │          │
            ╔═══════════╗     [s]─[t]
            ║  HUB  H1  ║──────────────╗
            ╚═════╦═════╝              ║
                  ║                    ║
            ╔═════╩═════╗        ╔═════╩═════╗
            ║  HUB  H2  ║════════║  HUB  H3  ║
            ╚═════╦═════╝        ╚═════╦═════╝
                  │                    │
    [e]─[f]───────┤              [u]───┤
     │            │               │    │
    [g]  [h]─[i]──┘          [v]─[w]  [x]─[y]─[z]

  Routing example: Signal from node [a] to node [z]
  
  WITHOUT hubs: [a]→[b]→[c]→???  (no direct path — many hops needed)
  
  WITH hubs:    [a]→[d]→H1→H3→[x]→[y]→[z]  (3 hops through hubs!)
  
  Modus Ponens via hubs:
    Hub H1 has learned: 'mammal → warm-blooded' (A→B rule)
    Signal arrives: 'whale is a mammal' (A is true)
    H1 fires: 'whale is warm-blooded' (therefore B)
    Routes conclusion to H3's biology cluster in ONE hop

  Connection distribution:
    24 regular nodes: avg 2-3 connections each
    3 hub nodes: 8-15 connections each
    → Power law: P(k) ~ k^(-γ), γ ≈ 2.5

children.title: Power Laws and Why Scale-Free Matters

children.children: In a uniform random graph (Erdős–Rényi), all nodes have roughly the same number of connections. In a scale-free graph, the degree distribution follows a power law: P(k) ~ k^(-γ). This means that while most nodes have very few connections, a tiny fraction have an enormous number.

This isn't just a structural curiosity — it has profound computational implications. Scale-free networks are ultra-small-world: the average path length scales as O(log log N) rather than O(log N) for regular small-world networks. For a network with a million nodes, that's the difference between ~4 hops and ~20 hops. Hub-based routing makes information propagation dramatically faster.

The trade-off is vulnerability: remove a hub and the network fragments. BDH would need redundancy across hubs to be robust. Whether this topology emerges naturally from training or must be pre-designed is an open question in the BDH proposal.

props.title: 6. Linear Attention Mapping: From O(N²) to O(N)

children.children: Standard transformer attention computes pairwise interactions between all tokens — O(N²) in sequence length. For a 100K token context, that's 10 billion attention computations per layer. BDH replaces this with a linear attention mechanism that maps the graph state into low-rank matrices U and V, achieving O(N) complexity.

# Standard Transformer Attention: O(N²)
def standard_attention(Q, K, V):
    # Q, K, V each have shape (N, d)
    scores = Q @ K.T          # N×N matrix — O(N²) compute and memory!
    weights = softmax(scores)  # N×N
    output = weights @ V       # N×d
    return output
    # For N=100,000: scores matrix = 10^10 entries 😱

# BDH Linear Attention Mapping: O(N)
def linear_attention(Q, K, V, phi):
    # phi() is a feature map (e.g., ELU+1, random features)
    Q_prime = phi(Q)  # N×r  (r << N, low rank)
    K_prime = phi(K)  # N×r
    
    # Key trick: compute K'ᵀV first → r×d matrix (SMALL!)
    KV = K_prime.T @ V   # r×d — O(N·r·d), linear in N
    Z = K_prime.T @ ones  # r×1 — normalization
    
    output = (Q_prime @ KV) / (Q_prime @ Z)  # N×d — O(N·r·d)
    return output
    # For N=100,000 with r=64: ~6.4M operations vs 10B 🎉

# The U and V matrices in BDH are analogous to K_prime and V:
# They summarize the entire graph state in low-rank form

Linear attention isn't unique to BDH — it's been explored in architectures like Performers, Linear Transformers, and RWKV. BDH's contribution is combining linear attention with the synaptic graph, using U and V matrices as a compressed bridge between the spike-based graph dynamics and attention-like sequence modeling.

Linear attention achieves O(N) by giving up the full N×N interaction matrix. This is an approximation — some long-range pairwise interactions that softmax attention would capture are lost or blurred. The quality of the approximation depends on the feature map φ and the rank r.

In practice, linear attention models have historically underperformed standard transformers on tasks requiring precise long-range retrieval (e.g., 'what was the 5th word of the document?'). BDH argues that synaptic state compensates for this — the graph's persistent memory can store precise associations that linear attention alone would lose. This is a plausible argument but remains to be empirically validated.

props.title: 7. The Four Stages of BDH Processing

children.children: BDH processes information through four sequential stages, each with a distinct computational role. Unlike transformer layers (which all do the same thing — attention + feedforward), BDH's stages are functionally specialized:

Input tokens
    │
    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STAGE 1: Input Encoding                │
│  (Sensory Steering)                     │
│                                         │
│  • Tokenize and embed input             │
│  • Route to relevant graph regions      │
│  • Like sensory cortex: 'what kind      │
│    of input is this?'                   │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STAGE 2: Synaptic Core                 │
│  (Scale-Free Graph Processing)          │
│                                         │
│  • Neuron particles fire/inhibit        │
│  • Hebbian learning updates edges       │
│  • Hub routers propagate signals        │
│  • Synaptic state σ evolves             │
│  • THIS IS WHERE THE MAGIC HAPPENS      │
│  • Like association cortex: 'what does  │
│    this mean?'                          │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STAGE 3: Linear Attention Mapping      │
│                                         │
│  • Graph state → low-rank U, V matrices │
│  • Sequence-level integration at O(N)   │
│  • Bridge between graph dynamics and    │
│    sequence modeling                    │
│  • Like working memory: 'hold this     │
│    together coherently'                 │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STAGE 4: Output Decoding               │
│  (Predictive Firing)                    │
│                                         │
│  • Generate next-token predictions      │
│  • Most-activated output neurons fire   │
│  • Like motor cortex: 'produce the      │
│    response'                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
    │
    ▼
Output tokens

The critical insight is that Stage 2 — the Synaptic Core — does double duty: it processes the current input AND updates its own structure via Hebbian learning. Every forward pass is also a learning pass. This is fundamentally different from transformers, where the forward pass is purely a read operation on fixed weights.

props.title: 8. BDH vs. Transformers: A Side-by-Side Comparison

children.children: Let's lay out the key architectural differences systematically:

┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
│    Property      │    Transformer       │    BDH               │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Attention        │ Global, all-to-all   │ Local + hub routing   │
│ Complexity       │ O(N²) per layer      │ O(N) linear mapping   │
│ Weights          │ Fixed at inference   │ Hebbian plasticity    │
│ Memory           │ External KV-cache    │ Synaptic state σ      │
│ Learning         │ Training only        │ Continuous (runtime)  │
│ Topology         │ Uniform layers       │ Scale-free graph      │
│ Activation       │ Dense (all neurons)  │ Sparse (clusters)     │
│ Context window   │ Fixed size           │ Persistent synaptic   │
│ Neuron model     │ Simple activation fn │ Integrate-and-fire    │
│ Scalability      │ Proven to 1T+ params │ Unproven at scale     │
│ Hardware support  │ Mature GPU/TPU stack │ Needs custom kernels  │
│ Benchmarks       │ SOTA on everything   │ No major benchmarks   │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘

Notice the last three rows. BDH's theoretical properties are intriguing, but transformers have an enormous practical advantage: they work, at scale, right now, with optimized hardware. BDH needs to demonstrate comparable or better performance on real tasks before its architectural advantages become meaningful.

BDH is in the 'early Tesla Roadster' phase — a proof of concept that shows a different approach is possible, but hasn't yet proven it can compete with the incumbent (transformers) on mainstream metrics. The theoretical advantages (continuous learning, sparse activation, persistent memory) are like the theoretical advantages of electric motors (instant torque, simpler drivetrain). But theory doesn't ship products — engineering does.
Intellectual honesty requires us to be clear about what BDH has NOT demonstrated:

1. SCALE: No evidence BDH works at transformer scale
   - Transformers: proven at 1T+ parameters, trillions of tokens
   - BDH: small-scale experiments only
   - Scaling laws are completely unknown

2. BENCHMARKS: No major benchmark results
   - No MMLU, HellaSwag, GSM8K, HumanEval scores
   - Without benchmarks, performance claims are aspirational

3. HEBBIAN STABILITY: Runtime learning may be fragile
   - Positive feedback loops can cause weight explosion
   - Catastrophic forgetting during long sessions
   - No proven homeostatic mechanism at scale

4. HARDWARE: Custom compute requirements
   - Transformers leverage highly optimized CUDA kernels
   - Sparse graph operations are hard to parallelize on GPUs
   - May need neuromorphic hardware to reach full potential

5. TRAINING: How do you train this?
   - Standard backprop doesn't apply cleanly to spiking networks
   - Surrogate gradient methods exist but add approximation error
   - Training instability is a known issue in spiking network research

AI has a long history of brain-inspired architectures that sounded promising but didn't pan out at scale. Hopfield networks, Boltzmann machines, Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), Hierarchical Temporal Memory (HTM) — all had elegant theoretical properties and biological motivation. All were eventually eclipsed by simpler, more scalable architectures (first CNNs/RNNs, then transformers).

This doesn't mean BDH will fail. But it means the burden of proof is high. The pattern in AI history is that engineering pragmatism beats biological elegance. BDH needs to show that its brain-inspired properties translate into measurable, practical advantages — not just theoretical ones.

The most important question for BDH isn't 'is the architecture elegant?' (it is) or 'is it biologically plausible?' (somewhat). It's 'does it produce better outputs than transformers on tasks people care about?' Until that's answered, BDH remains an interesting proposal, not a proven alternative.
BDH is open research. You can read the full paper and examine the code:

📄 Paper:  arXiv:2509.26507
   Title: 'Baby Dragon Hatchling: A Post-Transformer Architecture'
   URL:   https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.26507

💻 Code:   github.com/pathwaycom/bdh
   URL:   https://github.com/pathwaycom/bdh
   Language: Python (PyTorch)

🔑 Key things to look for in the code:
   - Neuron particle implementation (integrate-and-fire dynamics)
   - Hebbian learning rule and stability mechanisms
   - Scale-free graph construction and hub selection
   - Linear attention kernel (the U/V mapping)
   - Training pipeline and surrogate gradients

If you want to evaluate BDH yourself, the most useful experiment would be to run it on a standard benchmark (e.g., WikiText perplexity) and compare directly to a transformer of similar parameter count. If BDH can match transformer perplexity with fewer FLOPs or better sample efficiency, that's a meaningful result.

props.question: What is the primary advantage of BDH's synaptic state (σ) over a transformer's KV-cache?
options: Synaptic state persists and evolves during inference, while KV-cache is discarded between sessions
Synaptic state is faster to read
props.explanation: The key difference is persistence and plasticity. A transformer's KV-cache is a temporary buffer — it stores key-value pairs for the current session and is thrown away afterward. BDH's synaptic state lives on the graph edges and evolves continuously: connections strengthen when neurons co-fire (Hebbian learning) and persist across sessions. Memory isn't stored in a separate data structure — it IS the structure.

props.question: Why do hub nodes in BDH's scale-free graph enable faster reasoning?
options: Hub nodes have more parameters than regular nodes
Hub nodes use a different attention mechanism
Hub nodes connect to many clusters, enabling any-to-any information routing in few hops
Hub nodes store more memory than regular nodes
props.explanation: In a scale-free network, most nodes have few connections, but hub nodes connect to many clusters. This means information entering one part of the network can reach any other part in just 2-3 hops by routing through a hub — like the 'six degrees of separation' in social networks. For reasoning, this means a hub encoding 'A→B' (modus ponens) can rapidly route the conclusion 'B is true' when it receives the signal 'A is true,' connecting distant concept clusters without traversing the entire graph.

props.question: What is the biggest risk of Hebbian learning during inference?
options: Positive feedback loops can cause runaway weight growth and instability
It can only learn new vocabulary, not new concepts
props.explanation: Pure Hebbian learning has a well-known stability problem: if neuron A strengthens its connection to B, B fires more easily, which strengthens A→B further, creating a positive feedback loop. Weights can grow without bound. Biological brains solve this with homeostatic mechanisms (synaptic scaling, metaplasticity, inhibitory circuits). BDH proposes analogous mechanisms, but whether they're sufficient for stable operation over long inference sessions at scale is unproven.
